claims.claim_text: `gpt-5 mini` is faster and cheaper, and is a good fit for well-defined tasks.
claims.span_hint: section: Worked Decision Example: Support Ticket Triage
claims.rationale: The official model page directly states that GPT-5 mini is faster, more cost-efficient, and suited to well-defined tasks, but the primary source is on developers.openai.com, which is not currently allowlisted.

claims.claim_text: `gpt-5.2` is stronger for complex reasoning and agentic work.
claims.span_hint: section: Worked Decision Example: Support Ticket Triage
claims.rationale: OpenAI's GPT-5.2 launch page explicitly describes GPT-5.2 as built for professional work, long-running agents, tool use, and complex multi-step tasks.

claims.span_hint: section: Worked Decision Example: Support Ticket Triage
claims.rationale: The official GPT-5.2 model page includes current per-token pricing and a quick comparison showing GPT-5.2 priced above GPT-5 mini, but that primary source is on developers.openai.com rather than the current allowlist.

further_reading.title: GPT-5 Is Here
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for current OpenAI model-family positioning, pricing snapshots, and high-level model-selection context.

further_reading.title: Introducing GPT-5.2
further_reading.why_relevant: Relevant to the module's named-model comparison because it documents GPT-5.2's intended use cases, reasoning profile, and pricing.

further_reading.title: NIST AI Risk Management Framework
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful background for the module's validation, risk-gating, and human-review design guidance.

notes: Most of the module is pedagogical design guidance, hypothetical examples, schemas, quizzes, and exercises. I flagged only the explicit named-model capability and pricing comparisons as citation-requiring hard claims.
